title: Cellulitis
oneLiner: Acute bacterial skin infection of deeper dermis/subcutis; most common in lower limbs; can cause sepsis or hide necrotising fasciitis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – rapidly spreading deep fascial infection with systemic toxicity. **RED FLAG:** pain out of proportion to skin findings, crepitus, bullae, rapid progression (<24h), or extreme tenderness beyond the erythema border. *If suspected, immediate surgical review; do not wait for imaging.*
- **Sepsis / septic shock** – cellulitis can trigger overwhelming infection, especially in diabetics, immunocompromised, or elderly. **RED FLAG:** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, systolic BP <90, altered mental state. *Start resuscitation, IV antibiotics within 1 hour.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** unilateral limb redness, swelling, heat, pain; often with fever/chills, malaise.
- **Key exam findings:** poorly demarcated erythema (unlike erysipelas), warmth, oedema, tender to palpation; possible lymphangitis (red streaks), regional lymphadenopathy. Check for skin breaks (toe web fissures, ulcers, trauma). Check pulse and sensation – signs of compartment syndrome or ischaemia are not cellulitis.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Erysipelas** – superficial, well-demarcated, raised, intensely red plaque; often on face or legs; more common in young. *Splitter:* Erysipelas has a raised border; cellulitis has diffuse, non-raised erythema.
- **Deep vein thrombosis** – unilateral swelling, warmth, but usually less erythema; often Homan’s sign or history of immobility. *Wells’ score if low, do D-dimer; if high, do US.*
- **Stasis dermatitis** – bilateral lower limb, itching, dry skin, chronic venous changes, no fever. *Splitter:* No systemic symptoms, bilateral involvement, responds to elevation/emollients.
- **Gout / pseudogout** – monoarticular hot, red, swollen joint; often podagra; check serum urate, joint aspiration.
- **Superficial thrombophlebitis** – linear cord-like tenderness along a vein; often with varicose veins. *Ultrasound to rule out DVT extension.*
- **Contact dermatitis** – itchy, bilateral, with blistering; history of topical agent or plant exposure.
- **Lymphoedema** – chronic, non-pitting, bilateral; no fever or tenderness.
- **Systemic infection with skin signs** (e.g., shingles early, SSSS, drug eruption) – consider in atypical distribution or no portal of entry.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Mark the erythema border with a pen (to assess spread over time)
  - Check temperature, BP, HR, SpO2
  - Evaluate for lymphadenopathy, skin breaks, web space maceration
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, U&Es, LFT, creatinine, glucose (screen for diabetes)
  - Blood cultures – only if systemically unwell (NICE says not routinely); if septic, take 2 sets before antibiotics
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** None unless suspect DVT (US), foreign body (X-ray), or osteomyelitis (X-ray/MRI if underlying bone tenderness). For leg swelling, consider US Doppler if DVT suspected.
  - **Second-line:** MRI or CT if deep infection concern (e.g., necrotising fasciitis, gas in tissue)
- **Special tests:**
  - Swab from broken skin or pustules for MC&S – if exudate present (rarely helpful)
  - Anti-streptolysin O titre (ASOT) – not for acute diagnosis, for post-infection sequelae
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - No single validated score for cellulitis diagnosis. Use clinical judgment. Consider CURB-65 if pneumonia mimic (but not for cellulitis per se).
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Assessment for necrotising fasciitis** – the LRINEC score (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotizing Fasciitis) is a bedside risk score (Na, CRP, WBC, Hb, Cr, glucose). A score ≥8 suggests high risk. **But do not delay surgical review if clinical suspicion is high – imaging is not needed to rule out NF.**
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If NEWS2 ≥5 or septic: escalate sepsis 6 (oxygen, blood cultures, IV antibiotics, fluid bolus, monitor lactate, involve senior)
  - IV antibiotics: start empiric therapy within 1 hour if sepsis. First-line: [check local policy – typically IV flucloxacillin 1-2g QDS]. If penicillin-allergic: IV clindamycin 600mg QDS or IV teicoplanin.
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g QDS +/- codeine/NSAIDs (if no contraindication)
  - Elevate affected limb – reduces swelling – use pillows or a leg trough
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics until afebrile >48h and infection markers trending down. Then switch to oral: usually flucloxacillin 500mg QDS for 5-7 days total (extend if severe).
  - Monitor erythema border daily – measure and document. If expanding despite 24h of appropriate antibiotics, reconsider diagnosis (NF, staph aureus, atypical)
  - Ensure source control: if toe web fissures, treat with topical antifungal (clotrimazole) – often the portal of entry
  - Check urine dip (particularly if diabetic) to rule out concurrent UTI
  - VTE prophylaxis: anti-embolism stockings are contraindicated in acute leg cellulitis (increased swelling risk). Use prophylactic LMWH if immobile (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC daily)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Uncomplicated cellulitis in low-risk patients (no fever, no comorbidities, mild) can be managed orally with flucloxacillin 500mg QDS x7 days. Consider co-amoxiclav if broadened (but reserve for failure)
  - Prevention: treat underlying tinea pedis, manage oedema (compression stockings only after infection resolved), good foot hygiene
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: normal saline or Hartmann’s at maintenance (if no shock) – e.g., 30 mL/kg/day if euvolemic. If sepsis, give 500mL bolus stat, reassess.
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) + anti-embolism stockings *after* inflammation settles (not during acute phase – risk of pressure damage)
  - Diet: encourage oral intake; NBM only if surgical exploration planned
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (more frequent if unstable); record limb circumference daily at a fixed point
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **IV:** flucloxacillin 1-2g every 6 hours (if sepsis or severe). For penicillin allergy: clindamycin 600mg every 6 hours IV or teicoplanin (loading: 400mg BD on day 1 then 400mg OD). Duration: switch to oral when stable, total 7 days.
  - **Oral:** flucloxacillin 500mg every 6 hours (very severe cases may need higher – [check local policy])
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS PO / 1.2g TDS IV – covers Gram-negative rods (e.g., diabetic ulcers)
  - Doxycycline 100mg BD PO – if MRSA suspected (check local prevalence)
  - Clindamycin 300-450mg QDS PO – good for staph/strept; but risk of C. difficile
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Penicillin allergy: do not use flucloxacillin. Clindamycin caution in hepatic impairment.
  - NSAIDs: avoid if renal impairment, GI ulcer, or on anticoagulants. Paracetamol is safe.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Flucloxacillin: no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment. Prolonged use can cause hepatotoxicity (check LFTs).
  - Clindamycin: caution in hepatic impairment; dose reduction may be needed – [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **IV flucloxacillin – can cause phlebitis if given too fast or via small vein; also K+ content (4.5 mmol/g) – beware in renal failure. Always dilute and rotate sites.**
  - **Clindamycin – never forget to monitor for C. difficile diarrhoea; if fever recurs after initial improvement, consider C. diff.** 
  - **Gentamicin not used in cellulitis but if used for Gram-negative cover – must check levels; but generally avoided.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Necrotising fasciitis (surgical emergency)
  - Sepsis / septic shock
  - Abscess formation – may need incision and drainage
  - Lymphangitis, lymphadenitis
  - Deep vein thrombosis (unilateral leg swelling – consider)
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent cellulitis (often due to chronic oedema or lymphoedema)
  - Lymphoedema – permanent swelling after infection
  - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (rare, but group A strep can trigger after 2-3 weeks)
  - Chronic venous insufficiency exacerbated
sections.Prognosis: - Most cases resolve with antibiotics alone within 7-10 days. Mortality <1% in uncomplicated, but up to 10% if sepsis or underlying comorbidities. Recurrence rate ~30% in first year if risk factors (oedema, tinea pedis) not addressed.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Mark the border with a pen** – objective measure of progression; if it expands >24h despite ABx, reconsider diagnosis.
- **Always check the toe webs** – maceration and fungal infection are the most common portal of entry. Treat with topical antifungal (clotrimazole 1% cream) – prevents recurrence.
- **For recurrent leg cellulitis** – long-term prophylactic antibiotics (e.g., phenoxymethylpenicillin 250mg BD or erythromycin if allergic) after second episode – discuss with dermatology.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Using antibiotics in stasis dermatitis or gout** – these will not help and cause side effects. Use the splitter: no fever, bilateral, no portal of entry.
- **Delaying surgical opinion in suspected NF** – do not wait for imaging; if pain out of proportion, crepitus, or rapid spread, call the surgical registrar immediately. Do not rely on LRINEC score to rule out.
- **Prescribing NSAIDs in acute cellulitis** – some evidence suggests NSAIDs can mask progression to NF; use paracetamol first line.
- **Ordering anti-embolism stockings acutely** – can worsen swelling and cause pressure damage. Start VTE prophylaxis with LMWH only; defer stockings until swelling subsides.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Fever/chills recur or develop after 48h of oral antibiotics
  - Erythema expands beyond original marked border despite 24h of oral ABx
  - Severe pain, blistering, or dusky skin (necrotising fasciitis signs)
  - Difficulty walking or limb becomes cold/pale (compartment syndrome)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 48-72h if mild; if moderate, consider outpatient antibiotic therapy (OPAT) or daily review. For first episode, follow-up in 2 weeks to ensure resolution. Recurrent cases refer to dermatology (lymphoedema clinic or chronic cellulitis clinic).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2, BP <90 systolic despite fluid bolus (sepsis)
  - Any suspicion of necrotising fasciitis (pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, rapid progression) – immediate surgical referral. Do not wait for bloods or imaging.
  - Compartment syndrome – tense swelling, pain on passive stretch, reduced pulses – call orthopaedics/vascular for fasciotomy. 
  - GCS drop or confusion – may be septic encephalopathy.
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L with hypotension -> escalate to ICU outreach
  - LRINEC score ≥8 -> discuss with surgical team (even if no other signs)

rawMarkdown:
## Cellulitis
**One-liner:** Acute bacterial skin infection of deeper dermis/subcutis; most common in lower limbs; can cause sepsis or hide necrotising fasciitis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – rapidly spreading deep fascial infection with systemic toxicity. **RED FLAG:** pain out of proportion to skin findings, crepitus, bullae, rapid progression (<24h), or extreme tenderness beyond the erythema border. *If suspected, immediate surgical review; do not wait for imaging.*
- **Sepsis / septic shock** – cellulitis can trigger overwhelming infection, especially in diabetics, immunocompromised, or elderly. **RED FLAG:** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, systolic BP <90, altered mental state. *Start resuscitation, IV antibiotics within 1 hour.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** unilateral limb redness, swelling, heat, pain; often with fever/chills, malaise.
- **Key exam findings:** poorly demarcated erythema (unlike erysipelas), warmth, oedema, tender to palpation; possible lymphangitis (red streaks), regional lymphadenopathy. Check for skin breaks (toe web fissures, ulcers, trauma). Check pulse and sensation – signs of compartment syndrome or ischaemia are not cellulitis.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Erysipelas** – superficial, well-demarcated, raised, intensely red plaque; often on face or legs; more common in young. *Splitter:* Erysipelas has a raised border; cellulitis has diffuse, non-raised erythema.
- **Deep vein thrombosis** – unilateral swelling, warmth, but usually less erythema; often Homan’s sign or history of immobility. *Wells’ score if low, do D-dimer; if high, do US.*
- **Stasis dermatitis** – bilateral lower limb, itching, dry skin, chronic venous changes, no fever. *Splitter:* No systemic symptoms, bilateral involvement, responds to elevation/emollients.
- **Gout / pseudogout** – monoarticular hot, red, swollen joint; often podagra; check serum urate, joint aspiration.
- **Superficial thrombophlebitis** – linear cord-like tenderness along a vein; often with varicose veins. *Ultrasound to rule out DVT extension.*
- **Contact dermatitis** – itchy, bilateral, with blistering; history of topical agent or plant exposure.
- **Lymphoedema** – chronic, non-pitting, bilateral; no fever or tenderness.
- **Systemic infection with skin signs** (e.g., shingles early, SSSS, drug eruption) – consider in atypical distribution or no portal of entry.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Mark the erythema border with a pen (to assess spread over time)
  - Check temperature, BP, HR, SpO2
  - Evaluate for lymphadenopathy, skin breaks, web space maceration
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, U&Es, LFT, creatinine, glucose (screen for diabetes)
  - Blood cultures – only if systemically unwell (NICE says not routinely); if septic, take 2 sets before antibiotics
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** None unless suspect DVT (US), foreign body (X-ray), or osteomyelitis (X-ray/MRI if underlying bone tenderness). For leg swelling, consider US Doppler if DVT suspected.
  - **Second-line:** MRI or CT if deep infection concern (e.g., necrotising fasciitis, gas in tissue)
- **Special tests:**
  - Swab from broken skin or pustules for MC&S – if exudate present (rarely helpful)
  - Anti-streptolysin O titre (ASOT) – not for acute diagnosis, for post-infection sequelae
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - No single validated score for cellulitis diagnosis. Use clinical judgment. Consider CURB-65 if pneumonia mimic (but not for cellulitis per se).
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Assessment for necrotising fasciitis** – the LRINEC score (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotizing Fasciitis) is a bedside risk score (Na, CRP, WBC, Hb, Cr, glucose). A score ≥8 suggests high risk. **But do not delay surgical review if clinical suspicion is high – imaging is not needed to rule out NF.**

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If NEWS2 ≥5 or septic: escalate sepsis 6 (oxygen, blood cultures, IV antibiotics, fluid bolus, monitor lactate, involve senior)
  - IV antibiotics: start empiric therapy within 1 hour if sepsis. First-line: [check local policy – typically IV flucloxacillin 1-2g QDS]. If penicillin-allergic: IV clindamycin 600mg QDS or IV teicoplanin.
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g QDS +/- codeine/NSAIDs (if no contraindication)
  - Elevate affected limb – reduces swelling – use pillows or a leg trough
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics until afebrile >48h and infection markers trending down. Then switch to oral: usually flucloxacillin 500mg QDS for 5-7 days total (extend if severe).
  - Monitor erythema border daily – measure and document. If expanding despite 24h of appropriate antibiotics, reconsider diagnosis (NF, staph aureus, atypical)
  - Ensure source control: if toe web fissures, treat with topical antifungal (clotrimazole) – often the portal of entry
  - Check urine dip (particularly if diabetic) to rule out concurrent UTI
  - VTE prophylaxis: anti-embolism stockings are contraindicated in acute leg cellulitis (increased swelling risk). Use prophylactic LMWH if immobile (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC daily)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Uncomplicated cellulitis in low-risk patients (no fever, no comorbidities, mild) can be managed orally with flucloxacillin 500mg QDS x7 days. Consider co-amoxiclav if broadened (but reserve for failure)
  - Prevention: treat underlying tinea pedis, manage oedema (compression stockings only after infection resolved), good foot hygiene
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: normal saline or Hartmann’s at maintenance (if no shock) – e.g., 30 mL/kg/day if euvolemic. If sepsis, give 500mL bolus stat, reassess.
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) + anti-embolism stockings *after* inflammation settles (not during acute phase – risk of pressure damage)
  - Diet: encourage oral intake; NBM only if surgical exploration planned
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (more frequent if unstable); record limb circumference daily at a fixed point

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **IV:** flucloxacillin 1-2g every 6 hours (if sepsis or severe). For penicillin allergy: clindamycin 600mg every 6 hours IV or teicoplanin (loading: 400mg BD on day 1 then 400mg OD). Duration: switch to oral when stable, total 7 days.
  - **Oral:** flucloxacillin 500mg every 6 hours (very severe cases may need higher – [check local policy])
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS PO / 1.2g TDS IV – covers Gram-negative rods (e.g., diabetic ulcers)
  - Doxycycline 100mg BD PO – if MRSA suspected (check local prevalence)
  - Clindamycin 300-450mg QDS PO – good for staph/strept; but risk of C. difficile
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Penicillin allergy: do not use flucloxacillin. Clindamycin caution in hepatic impairment.
  - NSAIDs: avoid if renal impairment, GI ulcer, or on anticoagulants. Paracetamol is safe.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Flucloxacillin: no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment. Prolonged use can cause hepatotoxicity (check LFTs).
  - Clindamycin: caution in hepatic impairment; dose reduction may be needed – [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **IV flucloxacillin – can cause phlebitis if given too fast or via small vein; also K+ content (4.5 mmol/g) – beware in renal failure. Always dilute and rotate sites.**
  - **Clindamycin – never forget to monitor for C. difficile diarrhoea; if fever recurs after initial improvement, consider C. diff.** 
  - **Gentamicin not used in cellulitis but if used for Gram-negative cover – must check levels; but generally avoided.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Necrotising fasciitis (surgical emergency)
  - Sepsis / septic shock
  - Abscess formation – may need incision and drainage
  - Lymphangitis, lymphadenitis
  - Deep vein thrombosis (unilateral leg swelling – consider)
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent cellulitis (often due to chronic oedema or lymphoedema)
  - Lymphoedema – permanent swelling after infection
  - Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (rare, but group A strep can trigger after 2-3 weeks)
  - Chronic venous insufficiency exacerbated

### Prognosis
- Most cases resolve with antibiotics alone within 7-10 days. Mortality <1% in uncomplicated, but up to 10% if sepsis or underlying comorbidities. Recurrence rate ~30% in first year if risk factors (oedema, tinea pedis) not addressed.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Mark the border with a pen** – objective measure of progression; if it expands >24h despite ABx, reconsider diagnosis.
- **Always check the toe webs** – maceration and fungal infection are the most common portal of entry. Treat with topical antifungal (clotrimazole 1% cream) – prevents recurrence.
- **For recurrent leg cellulitis** – long-term prophylactic antibiotics (e.g., phenoxymethylpenicillin 250mg BD or erythromycin if allergic) after second episode – discuss with dermatology.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Using antibiotics in stasis dermatitis or gout** – these will not help and cause side effects. Use the splitter: no fever, bilateral, no portal of entry.
- **Delaying surgical opinion in suspected NF** – do not wait for imaging; if pain out of proportion, crepitus, or rapid spread, call the surgical registrar immediately. Do not rely on LRINEC score to rule out.
- **Prescribing NSAIDs in acute cellulitis** – some evidence suggests NSAIDs can mask progression to NF; use paracetamol first line.
- **Ordering anti-embolism stockings acutely** – can worsen swelling and cause pressure damage. Start VTE prophylaxis with LMWH only; defer stockings until swelling subsides.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Fever/chills recur or develop after 48h of oral antibiotics
  - Erythema expands beyond original marked border despite 24h of oral ABx
  - Severe pain, blistering, or dusky skin (necrotising fasciitis signs)
  - Difficulty walking or limb becomes cold/pale (compartment syndrome)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 48-72h if mild; if moderate, consider outpatient antibiotic therapy (OPAT) or daily review. For first episode, follow-up in 2 weeks to ensure resolution. Recurrent cases refer to dermatology (lymphoedema clinic or chronic cellulitis clinic).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2, BP <90 systolic despite fluid bolus (sepsis)
  - Any suspicion of necrotising fasciitis (pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, rapid progression) – immediate surgical referral. Do not wait for bloods or imaging.
  - Compartment syndrome – tense swelling, pain on passive stretch, reduced pulses – call orthopaedics/vascular for fasciotomy. 
  - GCS drop or confusion – may be septic encephalopathy.
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L with hypotension -> escalate to ICU outreach
  - LRINEC score ≥8 -> discuss with surgical team (even if no other signs)
